data.title: Boeing's stock falls as Air India crash raises more safety questions
data.description:
Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) stock fell as much as 5.9% on Thursday, while suppliers GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) and Spirit AeroSystems Holdings (NYSE:SPR) slipped as much as 2.8% and 3.4%, respectively, after one of Boeing’s 787 Dreamliners crashed in India on Thursday, likely killing everyone on board. The incident raises fresh questions about the safety of Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) planes.

The Air India flight, a 787-8 model on its way to London’s Gatwick Airport, went down shortly after departing from Ahmedabad, a major city in western India. The aircraft, which had 242 people on board, crashed into a residential neighborhood not far from the airport, triggering a massive emergency response, according to multiple press reports.

Air India said the flight took off at 1:38 p.m. local time and was carrying 230 passengers. Among them were 169 Indian nationals, 53 from the U.K., one Canadian and seven from Portugal.

“The injured are being taken to the nearest hospitals,” the airline said in a statement.

The Dreamliner, introduced in 2011, had maintained a strong safety record until this incident, with no previous fatal accidents.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) acknowledged the reports, stating,  “We are in contact with Air India regarding Flight 171 and stand ready to support them. Our thoughts are with the passengers, crew, first responders and all affected.”

GE Aerospace (NYSE:GE) is one of the manufacturers of jet engines for the 787, while Spirit AeroSystems (NYSE:SPR) produces main sections of the plane.

Footage from Indian news outlets showed thick black smoke billowing above the skyline and emergency workers transporting victims on stretchers. Flight data from Flightradar24 indicated the jet had reached just 625 feet in altitude and was descending at 475 feet per minute when its location signal was lost. The aircraft was 11 years old.

The crash site lies in Gujarat, a state known for its economic growth and home to many of India’s top business families. It is also the home state of Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi.

“It is heartbreaking beyond words. In this sad hour, my thoughts are with everyone affected by it,” Modi posted on X, formerly Twitter. He added that federal assistance was being provided to local officials in Ahmedabad.

Local police confirmed the plane crashed in a populated area after departing from the city’s main airport, according to several press reports.

Air India, once state-owned, has been operated by Tata Sons since 2022, after the Indian government divested the struggling airline. Natarajan Chandrasekaran, chairman of Air India, confirmed the accident in a message on X, writing that Flight 171 was “involved in a tragic accident today.”

High-profile safety incidents

This disaster adds to an already difficult year for the aviation sector, which has seen multiple high-profile incidents. In January, a passenger jet and a military helicopter collided near Washington, D.C.

Boeing (NYSE:BA) is still working to recover from the reputational damage caused by a widely publicized incident early last year, when a door panel detached mid-flight from a newly delivered 737 Max. That event led to the departure of the company’s then-CEO and triggered renewed concerns about Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) approach to safety and ongoing manufacturing flaws across its production facilities.

The aircraft involved in Thursday’s crash was not part of a recent delivery, it originally entered service in 2014, according to flight-tracking data from Flightradar24.

The Dreamliner, named through a public voting campaign that drew nearly 500,000 responses, is one of Boeing’s (BA) most popular widebody jets, with nearly 1,200 units delivered to date.

Despite its commercial success, the 787 has experienced its share of manufacturing challenges. It was hampered by supply chain delays and battery malfunctions in its early years, issues that led regulators to ground the entire fleet temporarily in 2013. More recently, Boeing (BA) suspended deliveries for almost two years due to quality-control concerns.

Production has since been ramped up, with Boeing (BA) executives reporting progress in streamlining manufacturing and enhancing workforce training. These efforts followed a slowdown intended to fix systemic issues on the factory floor.

Kelly Ortberg, who became chief executive in August, has warned employees that the company remains in a vulnerable position and cannot afford further missteps.

Earlier this month, Boeing (BA) agreed to pay $1.1 billion as part of a settlement to avoid criminal charges related to two earlier 737 Max crashes that occurred in 2018 and 2019, killing over 300 people. Under the terms of the deal, Boeing (BA) will allocate $455 million toward improving its safety, compliance and quality systems, and an additional $444.5 million will go to victims' families under a separate settlement agreement.

U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer expressed sympathy for those affected, saying, “The scenes emerging of a London-bound plane carrying many British nationals crashing in the Indian city of Ahmedabad are devastating.”

data.title: Perpetua Resources upsizes bought deal public offering to $325M; shares down 21%
data.description:
• Perpetua Resources (NASDAQ:PPTA) increased its bought deal offering to 24.62M shares at $13.20/share for ~$325M in gross proceeds.
  • Paulson & Co. will invest $100M via a concurrent private placement at the same offering price.
  • Underwriters have a 30-day option to buy up to 3.69M additional shares, potentially raising total gross proceeds to ~$374M.
  • Proceeds will support the Stibnite Gold Project and satisfy equity requirements for potential $2B EXIM Bank financing.
  • The offering is expected to close on or around June 16, 2025.
  • PPTA shares fall 21% on Thursday.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Arm CEO comes out against US export control curbs to China
data.description:
• Arm Holdings (NASDAQ:ARM) CEO Rene Haas has come out in full force against the U.S.'s export control curbs on advanced semiconductors to China, stating it's bad for consumers and companies alike.
  • “If you narrow access to technology and you force other ecosystems to grow up, it’s not good,” Haas told Bloomberg on at the Founders Forum Global conference. “It makes the pie smaller, if you will. And frankly, it’s not very good for consumers.”
  • Arm has a “quite significant” presence in China, Haas added.
  • Haas' comments echo those made by Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang. Last month, Huang said that the “export control was a failure,” adding that the fundamental assumptions that led to the AI diffusion rule proved to be “fundamentally flawed.”
  • The AI Diffusion rule, which was designed to limit the spread of advanced AI technology outside the U.S., with the purpose to prevent foreign threats from accessing it, was revoked last month.

data.title: HA Sustainable launches up to $500M cash tender offer for outstanding notes
data.description:
HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital (NYSE:HASI) announced on Thursday an up to $500M cash tender offer for 3.375% senior notes due 2026 and 8.00% green senior unsecured notes due 2027.

Shares were +0.55% during morning trade to $27.28.

The applicable offer consideration will be determined by reference to the fixed spread for the respective notes (100 basis points for notes due 2026 and 150 basis points for notes due 2027), and over the yield based on the bid-side price of the applicable Reference U.S. Treasury Security (4.125% U.S. Treasury due June 15, 2026, for notes due 2026 and 3.875% U.S. Treasury due May 31, 2027, for notes due 2027).

The price determination date is expected to be June 27.

The offer, expected to expire at 5:00 PM ET on July 14, includes an early tender premium of $30 per $1,000 principal amount.

data.title: BMO debuts U.S. equity ETF focused on human capital management
data.description:
Bank of Montreal (NYSE:BMO) is launching an exchange-traded fund investing in equity securities of U.S. issuers that possess strong corporate culture, it said on Thursday.

The BMO Human Capital Factor US Equity ETF (NEOE:ZHC:CA) will mainly invest in companies that score highly in the management of human capital, which seeks to link company culture and equity performance in a quantifiable factor.

The new fund has closed its initial offering of units and is listed and trading on Cboe Canada.

In the equity selection process, BMO Asset Management, as portfolio adviser, will use third party data and methodologies developed by Irrational Capital LLC that consider non-financial dimensions, such as direct managerial relationships and organizational alignment.

data.title: This Taiwanese hotpot stock is the biggest gainer in the restaurant sector over the last 30 days
data.description:
MasterBeef Group (NASDAQ:MB) gained 12.2% in early trading on Thursday and is now up more than 80% from its IPO pricing level in April. 

By ways of an introduction, MasterBeef Group (NASDAQ:MB) is a Hong Kong-based full-service restaurant company specializing in Taiwanese hotpot and barbecue cuisine. Founded in 2019, the company operates a network of restaurants and employs approximately 376 full-time staff. In addition to its core restaurant business, MasterBeef (MB) is pursuing diversification into the production and sale of semi-finished food products, such as packaged hotpot soup bases and marinated foods, as well as investing in technology solutions for operational efficiency.

According to Frost & Sullivan’s industry report, the MasterBeef (MB) brand ranked first among the specialty hotpot restaurant chain brands and Taiwanese hotpot restaurant chain brands in Hong Kong in terms of revenue, and the company also ranked first in the overall Taiwanese cuisine market in Hong Kong with a market share of approximately 9.7% in terms of revenue.

MasterBeef Group (MB) intends to use the IPO proceeds brought in for expansion of its restaurant network in Hong Kong and Southeast Asia, as well as general corporate purposes to support strategic growth.

Notably, MasterBeef Group (MB) has seen the best 30-day share price return in the restaurant sector as some investors have latched on to the restaurant growth story.

data.title: Micron expands US memory fab plans to $200B to achieve 40% domestic DRAM production
data.description:
Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) has expanded its fabrication and research and development plans in the U.S. to $200B to meet growing market demand and eventually produce 40% of its DRAM domestically, the company announced today.

The plan calls for building two high-volume fabs in Idaho, four high-volume fabs in New York, expanding and modernizing an existing fab in Virginia, and developing HBM packaging capabilities and R&D initiatives in the U.S. The first Idaho fab is slated for DRAM output in 2027, which will serve the growing artificial intelligence market.

Micron did not reveal when the New York fabs would enter production, but said the second Idaho fab would likely come online first. The memory maker has finalized $275M in funding through the CHIPS and Sciences Act, which will onshore Micron's 1-alpha DRAM node.

"Micron's U.S. memory manufacturing and R&D plans underscore our commitment to driving innovation and strengthening the domestic semiconductor industry," said Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra. 

"In partnership with the Department of Commerce, Micron is announcing a $200B semiconductor manufacturing and R&D investment to bring the full spectrum of memory chip production back to the United States," said U.S. Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick. "Micron's planned investment will ensure the U.S. advances its lead across critical industries like AI, automotive, and aerospace and defense."

Micron said its U.S. investments will be eligible for the Advanced Manufacturing Investment Credit, and the company has already secured support at the local, state and federal level. This includes up to $6.4B in CHIPS Act direct funding to support the construction of two Idaho fabs and two New York fabs, as well as the expansion and modernization of its Virginia fab.

"Micron's investment in expanding memory manufacturing and advanced packaging in the U.S. is a significant milestone for the semiconductor industry," said Amazon Web Services (AMZN) CEO Matt Garman.

The additional investments expand on Micron's plans dating from 2022 to increase its chipmaking footprint in the U.S.

data.title: Estée Lauder now available on Amazon's Canadian marketplace
data.description:
• Estée Lauder (NYSE:EL) cosmetics are now available to Amazon’s (AMZN) Canadian shoppers in the Amazon.ca Premium Beauty Store.
  • “This launch builds on our successful relationship with The Estée Lauder Companies and represents a significant milestone in our journey together. We've worked closely with the Estée Lauder team to create a shopping experience that honors the brand's rich heritage while making their beloved products more accessible to Canadian customers who appreciate the convenience of shopping with Amazon," VP of Amazon Canada Eva Lorenz said in a statement.
  • Estée Lauder launched the Amazon.com Premium Beauty Store last fall and merchandise purchased through Amazon.com and Amazon.ca receive free delivery for Prime members.
  • Estée Lauder shares are fractionally higher, erasing premarket losses.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: OpenAI ‘s Sam Altman predicts 2026 as a big year for AI
data.description:
OpenAI CEO Sam Altman predicted that 2026 could be a big year for scientific progress tied to advances in artificial intelligence technology.

Stargate, a collaboration of Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI, Oracle and SoftBank, will use a bankroll that could reach $500 billion to build massive installations across the U.S., said Altman, in a Bloomberg interview called The Circuit with Emily Chang.

After he spent many months selling the idea to prospective backers, Stargate started to come together last year, Altman said during the interview, adding that the project’s name stems from an early data center design that resembled the ring-shaped portal made famous in the eponymous movie and television series.

The rise of cost-efficient AI models like China-based DeepSeek and other companies inspired Stargate, Altman said.

“The area I’m most excited about is AI for scientific discovery,” Altman pointed out in the interview, adding that, “We’re not there yet—but we’re not far away.”

data.title: U.S. selected services revenue revised down slightly in Q1
data.description:
Q1 Quarterly Services Report: U.S. selected services total revenue rose 1.0% Q/Q to $5,829.0B, revised from the advance estimate of $5,830.2B, vs. Q4 2024's increase of 1.0%, the U.S. Census Bureau said on Thursday. The figure is up 5.6% from Q1 2024.

U.S. utilities revenue was $202.4B (revised from $216.5B), up 8.1% Q/Q (revised from +18.6%) and 10.0% Y/Y.

Transportation and warehousing revenue for Q1 2025 grew 0.8% Q/Q to $361.0B (revised from $343.0B), a 5.1% increase Y/Y.

Q1 information sector revenue increased 2.1% Q/Q and 6.9% Y/Y to $627.2B (revised from $617.0B).

Finance and insurance revenue rose 1.1% Q/Q and 6.0% Y/Y to $1,791.1B (revised from $1,790.5B).

Professional, scientific, and technical services revenue of $755.5B (revised from $742.7B), increased 0.4% Q/Q and 5.7% Y/Y.

Health care and social assistance revenue of $1,034.0B (revised from $1,026.5B), up 1.4% Q/Q and 6.8% Y/Y, the Census Bureau said.
